Mathematics.teks_description: Determine various types of discontinuities and explore limitations of graphing calculators
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers advanced limits for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(L)). Students should evaluate complex limits and understand the limitations of graphing technology. Advanced limit techniques: L'Hopital's rule for indeterminate forms (0/0 and ∞/∞), squeeze theorem (bounding a function between two that converge to the same limit), limits involving trigonometric functions (lim sin(x)/x = 1 as x→0), limits involving exponential functions. Types of indeterminate forms: 0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0^0, 1^∞, ∞^0. Epsilon-delta definition: formal definition of limit; for every ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that |f(x) - L| < ε whenever 0 < |x - c| < δ. Continuity: f is continuous at c if lim(x→c)f(x) = f(c); types of discontinuities revisited with rigorous definitions. Calculator limitations: may miss holes, vertical asymptotes can appear as lines, pixel limitations affect graph accuracy. Vocabulary: L'Hopital's rule, squeeze theorem, indeterminate form, epsilon-delta, continuity, piecewise, removable discontinuity, essential discontinuity, limit at infinity.

Mathematics.teks_description: Determine and analyze key features of functions including relative maximum, minimum, and zeros
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers advanced derivatives for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(I)). Students should apply differentiation techniques to analyze functions comprehensively. Advanced techniques: implicit differentiation (differentiate both sides with respect to x, solve for dy/dx), logarithmic differentiation (take ln of both sides for complex products/quotients), derivatives of inverse trig functions, parametric derivatives (dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt)). Advanced applications: curve sketching (first derivative test, second derivative test, concavity, inflection points), optimization (maximize/minimize real-world quantities), related rates (differentiate equations relating changing quantities with respect to time), linear approximation (tangent line approximation f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)), Newton's method for finding roots. Mean Value Theorem: if f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), there exists c where f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). Vocabulary: implicit differentiation, logarithmic differentiation, related rates, optimization, concavity, inflection point, Mean Value Theorem, linear approximation, Newton's method, critical point.

Mathematics.teks_description: Evaluate finite sums and geometric series when possible, written in sigma notation
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers advanced integration for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(A)). Students should master integration techniques and apply them to various problems. Techniques: u-substitution (reverse chain rule), integration by parts (∫u dv = uv - ∫v du), partial fractions (decompose rational functions), trigonometric substitution, trigonometric integrals. Applications: area between curves A = ∫[a,b] (f(x)-g(x))dx, volume of revolution (disk method V = π∫r²dx, washer method, shell method V = 2π∫xf(x)dx), arc length L = ∫√(1+(dy/dx)²)dx, surface area of revolution, work W = ∫F(x)dx, center of mass, probability. Improper integrals: infinite limits of integration or discontinuous integrands; evaluate as limits; convergent or divergent. Numerical integration: trapezoidal rule, Simpson's rule for approximation. Vocabulary: u-substitution, integration by parts, partial fractions, disk method, washer method, shell method, arc length, improper integral, convergent, divergent, trapezoidal rule, antiderivative.

Mathematics.teks_description: Generate and solve exponential equations in mathematical and real-world problems
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers introductory differential equations for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(I)). Students should solve basic differential equations and apply them to exponential models. A differential equation relates a function to its derivatives. Separable equations: dy/dx = f(x)g(y); separate variables and integrate both sides. First-order linear: dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x); use integrating factor e^(∫P dx). Exponential growth/decay: dy/dt = ky has solution y = y0e^(kt); k > 0 growth, k < 0 decay. Logistic growth: dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K); solution approaches carrying capacity K. Slope fields: visual representation of differential equation solutions; each point shows slope dy/dx; solution curves follow the field. Euler's method: numerical approximation; yn+1 = yn + h·f(xn,yn). Applications: population dynamics, radioactive decay, Newton's law of cooling, mixing problems, circuit analysis (RC circuits). Vocabulary: differential equation, separable, initial condition, particular solution, general solution, slope field, Euler's method, exponential growth, logistic growth, integrating factor, order.

Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; builds on Precalculus P.2 and P.5 foundations
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers multivariable calculus for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension beyond TEKS). Students should extend single-variable calculus concepts to functions of multiple variables. Functions of several variables: f(x,y) represents surfaces in 3D; domain is a region in the xy-plane; level curves (contour lines). Partial derivatives: ∂f/∂x (differentiate with respect to x, treat y as constant); ∂f/∂y similarly. Gradient: ∇f = <∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y>; points in direction of maximum increase; perpendicular to level curves. Chain rule for partial derivatives. Optimization: critical points where ∇f = 0; second derivative test using Hessian matrix determinant. Double integrals: ∫∫f(x,y)dA; iterated integrals; represent volume under surface. Changing order of integration; polar coordinates for circular regions. Triple integrals for volume. Applications: mass, center of mass, moment of inertia, probability. Vocabulary: partial derivative, gradient, level curve, contour plot, double integral, iterated integral, Jacobian, polar coordinates, cylindrical coordinates, spherical coordinates, critical point, saddle point.

Mathematics.skill: Vector calculus (vector fields, line integrals, surface integrals)
Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; builds on P.4(I) vectors and P.3(A) parametric equations
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers vector calculus for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should understand vector fields and integration along curves and over surfaces. Vector fields: F(x,y) = <P(x,y), Q(x,y)>; assign a vector to each point in space; examples include gravitational, electric, and fluid velocity fields. Line integrals: ∫C F·dr = ∫ (P dx + Q dy); compute work done by force along a path. Path independence: F is conservative if line integral depends only on endpoints; equivalent to F = ∇f for some scalar potential f; test: ∂P/∂y = ∂Q/∂x. Green's theorem: relates double integral over a region to line integral around its boundary; ∮(Pdx + Qdy) = ∫∫(∂Q/∂x - ∂P/∂y)dA. Surface integrals: integrate over surfaces in 3D; flux integrals ∫∫F·dS. Divergence theorem: relates flux through closed surface to triple integral of divergence. Stokes' theorem: relates line integral to surface integral of curl. Vocabulary: vector field, line integral, surface integral, conservative, potential function, curl, divergence, Green's theorem, Stokes' theorem, divergence theorem, flux, circulation.

Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; builds on 2A.3 systems and matrix operations
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers linear algebra for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should understand vector spaces, linear transformations, and eigenvalues. Vector spaces: sets with vector addition and scalar multiplication satisfying axioms; examples include R^n, polynomial spaces, function spaces. Subspaces: subsets that are themselves vector spaces. Linear independence: vectors are independent if no vector is a linear combination of others. Basis: linearly independent spanning set; dimension = number of basis vectors. Linear transformations: T:V→W preserving addition and scalar multiplication; represented by matrices. Matrix operations: row reduction, rank (dimension of column space), nullity (dimension of null space), rank-nullity theorem. Eigenvalues and eigenvectors: Av = λv; found by solving det(A - λI) = 0 for λ, then (A - λI)v = 0 for v. Diagonalization: A = PDP^(-1) when n linearly independent eigenvectors exist. Applications: systems of differential equations, Markov chains, Google PageRank, computer graphics, quantum mechanics. Vocabulary: vector space, subspace, linear independence, basis, dimension, linear transformation, eigenvalue, eigenvector, determinant, rank, nullity, diagonalization, kernel, image.

Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; advanced abstract mathematics
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers introductory abstract algebra for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students explore algebraic structures that generalize familiar number systems. Groups: a set G with a binary operation satisfying closure, associativity, identity element, and inverse element. Examples: integers under addition, nonzero rationals under multiplication, symmetry groups (dihedral groups, symmetric groups), cyclic groups. Subgroups: subsets that form groups under the same operation; Lagrange's theorem (order of subgroup divides order of group). Group homomorphisms: structure-preserving maps between groups; isomorphisms are bijective homomorphisms. Rings: sets with two operations (addition forming abelian group, multiplication being associative with distributive property). Commutative rings, rings with unity, integral domains. Fields: commutative rings where every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse; examples include Q, R, C, finite fields. Applications: cryptography (RSA uses number theory and group theory), coding theory, crystallography, physics. Vocabulary: group, ring, field, binary operation, identity, inverse, closure, associativity, commutativity, subgroup, homomorphism, isomorphism, cyclic group, abelian group.

Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; advanced number theory topics
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers number theory for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students explore properties of integers and their applications. Divisibility: a|b means b = ka for some integer k; division algorithm b = qa + r (0 ≤ r < a). GCD: greatest common divisor; Euclidean algorithm for efficient computation; Bezout's identity ax + by = gcd(a,b). Prime numbers: integers > 1 with no positive divisors other than 1 and themselves; Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic (unique prime factorization); infinitely many primes (Euclid's proof). Modular arithmetic: a ≡ b (mod n) means n|(a-b); arithmetic with remainders; useful for cryptography and computer science. Fermat's little theorem: a^p ≡ a (mod p) for prime p. Euler's theorem: a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) when gcd(a,n) = 1; φ is Euler's totient function. Chinese Remainder Theorem. Applications: RSA encryption (relies on difficulty of factoring large numbers), hash functions, error-detecting codes, digital signatures. Vocabulary: divisibility, prime, composite, GCD, LCM, Euclidean algorithm, modular arithmetic, congruence, Fermat's theorem, Euler's totient, Chinese Remainder Theorem, RSA.

Mathematics.teks_description: Discrete Mathematics for Computer Science - TEKS Chapter 126 Technology Applications
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers discrete mathematics for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS §126.37). Students should understand graph theory and combinatorics for computer science applications. Graph theory: graphs consist of vertices and edges; types include directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted, connected/disconnected, cyclic/acyclic. Key concepts: degree of vertex, paths, cycles, Euler paths/circuits (traverse every edge once), Hamiltonian paths/circuits (visit every vertex once), trees (connected acyclic graphs), graph coloring, planar graphs. Algorithms: BFS, DFS, Dijkstra's shortest path, minimum spanning tree (Kruskal's, Prim's). Combinatorics: permutations (order matters, P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!), combinations (order doesn't matter, C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n-r)!)), pigeonhole principle, inclusion-exclusion, binomial theorem. Recurrence relations: Fibonacci sequence, solving linear recurrences. Applications: network design, scheduling, circuit design, social networks, data structures, algorithm analysis, coding theory. Vocabulary: graph, vertex, edge, degree, path, cycle, tree, Euler, Hamiltonian, planar, permutation, combination, binomial coefficient, recurrence relation, algorithm.

Mathematics.teks_description: Analyze situations modeled by functions to solve real-world problems
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers mathematical modeling for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(N)). Students should analyze real-world situations and create mathematical models to solve problems. Modeling process: identify the problem, make simplifying assumptions, formulate a mathematical model (equation, function, system), solve mathematically, interpret results in context, validate and refine the model. Model types: linear (constant rate of change), exponential (constant percentage change), logistic (bounded growth), polynomial (multiple turning points), periodic/trigonometric (cyclical phenomena). Key concepts: choosing appropriate model type based on data behavior, fitting models to data using technology (regression), evaluating model quality (R², residual analysis), recognizing model limitations and domain restrictions, sensitivity analysis (how changes in parameters affect output). Real-world applications: population growth, epidemic modeling (SIR model), financial planning, physics simulations, climate models, optimization of resources. Vocabulary: model, assumption, parameter, variable, fit, residual, interpolation, extrapolation, validation, sensitivity analysis, simulation, deterministic, stochastic.

Mathematics.teks_description: Calculate confidence intervals for population means with known standard deviation
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers calculus-based statistics for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.6(C)). Students should apply calculus to probability and construct confidence intervals. Continuous probability distributions: probability density function (pdf) f(x) where P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = ∫[a,b] f(x)dx; total area = 1. Expected value: E(X) = ∫xf(x)dx. Variance: Var(X) = E(X²) - [E(X)]². Normal distribution: f(x) = (1/(σ√(2π)))e^(-(x-μ)²/(2σ²)); 68-95-99.7 rule; z-scores standardize. Confidence intervals for population mean: x̄ ± z*(σ/√n) when σ known; x̄ ± t*(s/√n) when σ unknown. Hypothesis testing: null and alternative hypotheses, test statistic, p-value, significance level α, Type I and Type II errors. Chi-squared tests for categorical data. Central Limit Theorem: sampling distribution of x̄ approaches normal as n increases regardless of population shape. Vocabulary: probability density function, expected value, variance, normal distribution, confidence interval, hypothesis test, significance level, p-value, Type I error, Type II error, Central Limit Theorem, chi-squared.

Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; advanced complex number analysis
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers introductory complex analysis for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should explore functions of complex variables and their properties. Complex functions: f(z) where z = x + iy; maps the complex plane to the complex plane. Elementary functions: exponential e^z = e^x(cos y + i sin y), trigonometric (defined via exponential), logarithmic (multi-valued: log z = ln|z| + i(arg z + 2πn)). Analytic functions: complex differentiable; Cauchy-Riemann equations ∂u/∂x = ∂v/∂y and ∂u/∂y = -∂v/∂x characterize analyticity. Contour integrals: ∮f(z)dz along paths in the complex plane. Cauchy's integral theorem: ∮f(z)dz = 0 for analytic functions on simply connected domains. Residue theorem: relates contour integrals to residues at singularities. Power series: Taylor and Laurent series for complex functions. Applications: fluid dynamics, electrostatics, signal processing, evaluating real integrals. Vocabulary: analytic function, Cauchy-Riemann equations, contour integral, residue, singularity, pole, branch cut, conformal mapping, Laurent series, holomorphic.

Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; rigorous foundations of calculus
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers introductory real analysis for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should understand rigorous foundations of calculus. Completeness: the real numbers have no gaps (every Cauchy sequence converges, every bounded set has a supremum). Sequences: convergence (for every ε > 0, there exists N such that |an - L| < ε for n > N), Cauchy sequences, monotone convergence theorem, Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem. Series: convergence tests (comparison, ratio, root, integral, alternating series tests), absolute vs. conditional convergence. Continuity: epsilon-delta definition, intermediate value theorem, extreme value theorem on closed intervals. Differentiability: formal definition, differentiability implies continuity (but not vice versa), Mean Value Theorem with proof, Taylor's theorem with remainder. Integration: Riemann integral as limit of Riemann sums, integrability of continuous functions, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (rigorous statement and proof). Key concepts: proof techniques (direct, contradiction, induction, contrapositive), logical rigor, mathematical maturity. Vocabulary: supremum, infimum, convergence, Cauchy sequence, completeness, epsilon-delta, uniform continuity, pointwise convergence, uniform convergence, compactness.

Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; advanced mathematical structures
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers introductory topology for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should explore fundamental concepts of topological spaces and continuous deformations. Topology: study of properties preserved under continuous deformations (stretching, bending, but not tearing or gluing). Open and closed sets: open set contains a neighborhood around each point; closed set contains all its limit points; a set can be both, neither, open, or closed. Topological spaces: set X with a collection of open sets satisfying axioms (empty set and X are open, arbitrary unions of open sets are open, finite intersections of open sets are open). Metric spaces: topology induced by a distance function. Homeomorphism: continuous bijection with continuous inverse; topologically equivalent spaces (donut ≈ coffee cup). Key properties: connectedness (cannot be split into two disjoint open sets), compactness (every open cover has a finite subcover), Hausdorff property. Euler characteristic: V - E + F for polyhedra; topological invariant. Fundamental group: algebraic structure capturing loops in a space. Applications: data analysis (topological data analysis), network theory, physics, robotics, computer graphics. Vocabulary: topology, open set, closed set, neighborhood, continuous, homeomorphism, connectedness, compactness, metric space, manifold, Euler characteristic, genus.

Mathematics.teks_description: Explore limitations of graphing calculators and numerical methods for approximation
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers numerical analysis for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(L)). Students should understand computational methods for approximating mathematical solutions and the limitations of graphing technology. Root-finding: bisection method (reliable but slow), Newton's method (fast but may not converge), secant method. Interpolation: Lagrange interpolation, Newton's divided differences, spline interpolation for smooth curves. Numerical integration: rectangle rule, trapezoidal rule, Simpson's rule; error analysis for each method. Numerical differentiation: finite difference approximations (forward, backward, central differences). Solving differential equations: Euler's method (first-order), improved Euler (Heun's method), Runge-Kutta methods (higher accuracy). Error analysis: truncation error (from method approximation), round-off error (from finite precision), stability, convergence, and accuracy trade-offs. Calculator/computer limitations: floating-point representation, loss of significance in subtraction, overflow/underflow. Vocabulary: numerical method, approximation, convergence, stability, truncation error, round-off error, iteration, algorithm, interpolation, extrapolation, Newton's method, Euler's method, Simpson's rule.

Mathematics.teks_description: Apply mathematics to problems arising in everyday life, society, and the workplace
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers the history of mathematics for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS A.1(A)). Students should understand how mathematical ideas developed and their impact on everyday life, society, and the workplace. Ancient mathematics: Babylonian (base-60 system, quadratic equations), Egyptian (practical geometry, fractions), Greek (axiomatic reasoning, Euclid's Elements, Pythagorean theorem, proof concept, Archimedes' contributions). Medieval contributions: Islamic mathematicians (algebra from al-Khwarizmi, decimal system transmission), Indian mathematicians (zero as a number, negative numbers, early calculus concepts by Madhava). Renaissance and early modern: Fibonacci (Hindu-Arabic numerals in Europe), development of symbolic algebra, Descartes (coordinate geometry), Fermat and Pascal (probability). Calculus: Newton and Leibniz independently developed calculus; controversy over priority. Modern mathematics: Gauss (number theory), Euler (analysis, graph theory), Riemann (geometry, integration), Cantor (set theory, infinity), Godel (incompleteness theorems), Turing (computation theory). Impact: mathematics drives technology, science, engineering, economics, cryptography, AI. Vocabulary: axiom, proof, algebra, geometry, calculus, number theory, set theory, computation, algorithm, mathematical notation.

Mathematics.teks_description: Write exponential functions describing growth and decay including compound interest models
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers financial mathematics for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS A.9(C)). Students should apply exponential functions to financial models including compound interest. Simple interest: I = Prt, A = P(1 + rt). Compound interest: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt) where P = principal, r = annual rate, n = compounding frequency, t = years. Continuous compounding: A = Pe^(rt). Effective annual rate: reff = (1 + r/n)^n - 1. Annuities: regular payments; future value FV = PMT × ((1+r/n)^(nt) - 1)/(r/n); present value PV = PMT × (1 - (1+r/n)^(-nt))/(r/n). Loan amortization: calculate monthly payment, create amortization schedule showing principal and interest portions. Key concepts: time value of money (dollar today worth more than dollar tomorrow), NPV (net present value) for investment decisions, APR vs. APY, Rule of 72 (doubling time ≈ 72/rate%). Applications: savings plans, mortgage calculations, retirement planning, credit card debt, bond valuation, stock options basics. Vocabulary: principal, interest rate, compound interest, annuity, amortization, present value, future value, APR, APY, effective rate, time value of money.

Mathematics.teks_description: Compare statistical measures from simulated sampling distributions to theoretical distributions
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers actuarial science for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.5(D)). Students should apply statistical distributions to risk assessment and compare simulated to theoretical distributions. Actuarial science: uses mathematics, statistics, and financial theory to study uncertain future events, especially in insurance and pension contexts. Key concepts: probability distributions for modeling risk (normal, binomial, Poisson, exponential), expected value as measure of average outcome, variance and standard deviation as measures of risk, law of large numbers (actual results converge to expected value with large samples). Life tables: mortality rates by age, life expectancy calculations, survival functions. Insurance principles: premium calculation (expected loss + expenses + profit margin), pooling of risk, diversification, moral hazard, adverse selection. Simulation: Monte Carlo methods generate random scenarios to estimate probabilities and outcomes; compare simulation results to theoretical distributions. Applications: life insurance pricing, pension fund management, health insurance, property/casualty insurance, financial risk management. Vocabulary: actuary, risk, premium, mortality rate, life table, expected value, variance, probability distribution, Monte Carlo simulation, law of large numbers, adverse selection, moral hazard.

Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; optimization and decision science
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers operations research for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should apply mathematical optimization techniques to decision-making problems. Linear programming: maximize or minimize a linear objective function subject to linear inequality constraints; feasible region is a convex polygon; optimal solution at a vertex (corner point method). Simplex method: algebraic approach for larger problems. Integer programming: when variables must be whole numbers. Key concepts: formulating optimization problems, identifying constraints and objective functions, sensitivity analysis (how changes in parameters affect optimal solution). Network optimization: shortest path (Dijkstra's algorithm), minimum spanning tree, maximum flow, transportation and assignment problems. Queuing theory: modeling waiting lines; arrival rates, service rates, queue length, waiting time. Decision theory: decision trees, expected monetary value, utility theory, game theory (Nash equilibrium, zero-sum games). Scheduling: critical path method (CPM), PERT for project management. Applications: supply chain logistics, airline scheduling, resource allocation, inventory management, military planning. Vocabulary: optimization, linear programming, objective function, constraint, feasible region, simplex, integer programming, network, queue, decision tree, game theory, Nash equilibrium.

Mathematics.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; builds on number theory and discrete math foundations
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers cryptography for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension building on number theory and discrete math). Students should understand mathematical foundations of secure communication. Classical ciphers: Caesar cipher (shift), Vigenere cipher (polyalphabetic), substitution ciphers; vulnerable to frequency analysis. Symmetric encryption: same key for encryption and decryption; AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) widely used; fast but key distribution problem. Asymmetric (public-key) encryption: RSA algorithm uses difficulty of factoring large numbers; public key encrypts, private key decrypts; based on Euler's theorem and modular arithmetic. RSA process: choose large primes p, q; compute n = pq, φ(n) = (p-1)(q-1); choose e coprime to φ(n); compute d = e^(-1) mod φ(n); public key (n,e), private key (n,d). Diffie-Hellman key exchange: allows two parties to establish shared secret over insecure channel; based on discrete logarithm problem. Hash functions: one-way functions producing fixed-length output; used for digital signatures, password storage, data integrity (SHA-256). Quantum computing threat: Shor's algorithm could break RSA; post-quantum cryptography research. Vocabulary: encryption, decryption, cipher, plaintext, ciphertext, key, RSA, public key, private key, modular arithmetic, hash function, digital signature, Diffie-Hellman, quantum computing.

Science.skill: Advanced topics in Biology, Chemistry, and Physics (depending on student's chosen path)
Science.teks_description: Relate how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society
Science.prompt: This topic covers advanced science topics for 12th grade Science students (TEKS B.4(B)). Students should understand how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society. At the 12th grade level, students pursue specialized pathways in biology, chemistry, or physics, connecting classroom knowledge to current research frontiers. Advanced biology: CRISPR applications, epigenomics, microbiome research, neuroscience, bioinformatics. Advanced chemistry: computational chemistry, green chemistry, nanotechnology, materials science, drug design. Advanced physics: particle physics, cosmology, quantum computing, gravitational waves, fusion energy research. Key concepts: reading and interpreting primary research literature, understanding peer review, identifying research gaps, connecting fundamental science to technological applications, science communication skills. Students should evaluate how breakthrough discoveries (DNA structure, general relativity, quantum mechanics) transformed scientific paradigms. Vocabulary: paradigm shift, peer review, primary literature, research methodology, frontier research, interdisciplinary, translational research, innovation.

Science.teks_description: Analyze, evaluate, and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence, logical reasoning, and experimental testing
Science.prompt: This topic covers research projects for 12th grade Science students (TEKS B.4(A)). Students should demonstrate mastery of scientific inquiry by designing and conducting independent research. Research process: identify a researchable question, conduct literature review, formulate testable hypothesis, design experimental methodology (controls, variables, sample size, statistical analysis plan), collect data, analyze results using appropriate statistical methods, draw evidence-based conclusions, communicate findings. Key concepts: research ethics (IRB protocols, informed consent, data integrity), experimental design principles, statistical analysis (t-tests, ANOVA, chi-square, correlation, regression), scientific writing (abstract, introduction, methods, results, discussion format), presentation skills. Students should understand the difference between experimental and observational studies, control for confounding variables, and assess the significance and limitations of their findings. Science fair participation or independent research mentorship with professionals provides authentic experience. Vocabulary: hypothesis, experimental design, control, variable, statistical significance, literature review, methodology, data analysis, conclusion, peer review, IRB, abstract.

Science.teks_description: Research STEM careers through museums, libraries, organizations, companies, and mentors
Science.prompt: This topic covers independent study in science for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(C)). Students should research STEM careers through museums, libraries, professional organizations, companies, and mentors. This self-directed learning experience allows students to explore a scientific topic of personal interest in depth. Key components: selecting a focused topic, developing a research plan with timeline and milestones, working with a mentor (teacher, university professor, industry professional), maintaining a research journal, producing a final product (paper, presentation, portfolio, prototype). Career exploration: identifying STEM career pathways, understanding educational requirements, gaining awareness of professional skills needed, networking with professionals. Resources: scientific journals (Nature, Science, PNAS), databases (PubMed, Google Scholar), professional organizations (ACS, APS, AAAS), university partnerships, internship opportunities. Key concepts: self-directed learning, time management, professional communication, developing expertise in a specialized area, connecting classroom learning to real-world applications. Vocabulary: independent study, mentor, research plan, STEM career, professional development, portfolio, primary source, database, internship, networking.

Science.skill: AP or IB level courses in science
Science.teks_description: Advanced Placement or International Baccalaureate coursework; extends beyond standard TEKS
Science.prompt: This topic covers AP and IB level science courses for 12th grade students. These advanced courses extend beyond standard TEKS, providing college-level rigor. AP courses: AP Biology (molecular biology, genetics, ecology, evolution at college depth), AP Chemistry (thermodynamics, equilibrium, kinetics, electrochemistry with quantitative emphasis), AP Physics C (calculus-based mechanics and electricity/magnetism), AP Environmental Science (earth systems, resources, pollution). IB courses: Higher Level (HL) courses require deeper understanding and additional topics compared to Standard Level (SL); Internal Assessment (IA) requires original experimental investigation. Key concepts: college-level problem solving, laboratory skills, extended response writing, independent research, preparation for college placement exams. Students should develop skills in quantitative analysis, scientific argumentation, experimental design, and critical reading of scientific literature. These courses prepare students for success in college-level science and competitive STEM programs. Vocabulary: AP exam, IB assessment, college credit, laboratory investigation, free response, multiple choice, internal assessment, extended essay, college readiness.

Science.skill: Specialized science courses (e.g., anatomy, physiology, astronomy, environmental science, forensic science)
Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C includes specialized HS science electives
Science.prompt: This topic covers specialized science electives for 12th grade students (TEKS §112.C). Students can pursue in-depth study in specific scientific disciplines. Anatomy and Physiology: detailed study of human body structure and function; organ systems, tissue types, pathophysiology; preparation for healthcare careers. Astronomy: study of celestial objects, stellar evolution, cosmology, observational techniques; connects physics to space science. Environmental Science: ecosystems, sustainability, pollution, natural resources, environmental policy; interdisciplinary approach. Forensic Science: application of science to law; evidence collection and analysis, DNA fingerprinting, toxicology, ballistics, crime scene investigation. Additional electives: Aquatic Science, Earth and Space Science, Engineering Design. Key concepts: applying core science principles to specialized contexts, developing career-relevant skills, understanding interdisciplinary connections between sciences. These courses provide hands-on, applied learning that connects theoretical knowledge to real-world professional practice. Vocabulary: specialization, elective, applied science, interdisciplinary, career pathway, laboratory technique, field study, case study.

Science.teks_description: Relate how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society
Science.prompt: This topic covers science-technology-society connections for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(B)). Students should understand how scientific research impacts technology development and societal change. Historical examples: Pasteur's germ theory transformed medicine and public health; Haber-Bosch process enabled synthetic fertilizers (feeding billions but causing environmental damage); discovery of DNA structure led to biotechnology revolution; transistor invention enabled computer revolution. Current connections: AI and machine learning, gene editing (CRISPR ethical debates), climate science and policy, renewable energy technology, pandemic response and vaccine development, space exploration (Mars missions, commercial spaceflight). Key concepts: technology as applied science, unintended consequences of technological development, science policy and funding, public understanding of science, responsible innovation, science communication. Students should analyze how societal needs drive scientific research and how scientific discoveries create new technological capabilities and social challenges. Vocabulary: innovation, technology transfer, unintended consequences, science policy, responsible research, science communication, public engagement, ethical implications, societal impact.

Science.teks_description: Relate how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society including ethical considerations
Science.prompt: This topic covers ethics in science for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(B)). Students should understand ethical considerations in scientific research and their societal implications. Research ethics: honesty and integrity in data reporting, proper attribution and avoiding plagiarism, informed consent for human subjects (IRB review), humane treatment of animal subjects (IACUC review), conflicts of interest disclosure. Bioethics: genetic engineering debates (germline vs. somatic editing, designer babies), stem cell research, cloning, end-of-life decisions, organ transplantation allocation. Environmental ethics: anthropocentrism vs. ecocentrism, intergenerational responsibility, environmental justice (disproportionate impacts on marginalized communities), precautionary principle. Dual-use research: research with potential for both beneficial and harmful applications (gain-of-function virology, nuclear physics, AI). Key concepts: scientific integrity, responsible conduct of research, whistleblowing, data fabrication/falsification, scientific misconduct consequences, balancing innovation with precaution. Vocabulary: bioethics, research ethics, informed consent, IRB, IACUC, plagiarism, conflict of interest, dual-use, precautionary principle, environmental justice, scientific misconduct.

Science.teks_description: Relate how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society
Science.prompt: This topic covers current events in science for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(B)). Students should connect current scientific developments to foundational principles and evaluate their societal significance. Current areas of rapid advancement: artificial intelligence and machine learning (large language models, autonomous vehicles, drug discovery), gene editing (CRISPR clinical trials for sickle cell disease, cancer immunotherapy), climate science (IPCC reports, tipping points, carbon capture technologies, renewable energy breakthroughs), space exploration (James Webb Space Telescope discoveries, Mars missions, commercial spaceflight, asteroid mining), pandemic preparedness (mRNA vaccine technology, antiviral development, genomic surveillance), quantum computing (quantum supremacy claims, practical applications). Key skills: reading and evaluating science news (distinguishing reliable sources from misinformation), understanding peer review process, recognizing media bias in science reporting, evaluating claims using evidence-based reasoning. Students should regularly read science news (Nature, Science, Scientific American) and discuss implications. Vocabulary: peer review, preprint, meta-analysis, scientific consensus, science journalism, misinformation, evidence-based, breakthrough, clinical trial, paradigm.

Science.teks_description: Research STEM careers and preparation through organizations, companies, and mentors
Science.prompt: This topic covers preparation for college science for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(C)). Students should develop skills and knowledge needed for success in college-level STEM courses. Academic skills: reading primary scientific literature, scientific writing (lab reports, research papers), quantitative reasoning, problem-solving strategies, time management, study skills specific to science courses. Laboratory skills: proper technique, safety protocols, equipment use, data collection and recording, error analysis, lab report writing. Mathematical preparation: calculus applications in physics and chemistry, statistical analysis, graphing and data visualization, dimensional analysis, unit conversions. Career awareness: exploring STEM fields through research, internships, and mentorship; understanding educational pathways (bachelor's, master's, PhD, professional degrees); connecting coursework to career goals. College transition: understanding expectations differences (pace, independence, depth), developing self-advocacy skills, utilizing office hours and tutoring, collaborative learning. Key resources: AP and IB exam preparation, SAT Subject Tests, college application guidance, scholarship opportunities, summer research programs (REU). Vocabulary: undergraduate, STEM, research experience, laboratory skills, scientific literacy, academic writing, study skills, mentorship, internship, career pathway.

Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Specialized HS science - Earth and Space Science standards
Science.prompt: This topic covers astronomy for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand celestial objects, their properties, and the methods used to study them. Observational astronomy: telescopes (refracting, reflecting, radio), electromagnetic spectrum observations, space-based observatories (Hubble, James Webb). Stellar astronomy: stellar classification (spectral types OBAFGKM), Hertzsprung-Russell diagram (main sequence, giants, supergiants, white dwarfs), stellar evolution (protostar → main sequence → red giant → planetary nebula/supernova → white dwarf/neutron star/black hole). Solar system: formation (solar nebula hypothesis), planetary types (terrestrial, gas giants, ice giants), moons, asteroids, comets, Kuiper Belt, Oort Cloud. Exoplanets: detection methods (transit, radial velocity, direct imaging), habitable zone, search for biosignatures. Key concepts: light-year as distance unit, redshift and expanding universe, spectroscopy for composition analysis, gravitational effects on celestial motion. Vocabulary: telescope, spectroscopy, luminosity, magnitude, main sequence, red giant, white dwarf, neutron star, black hole, exoplanet, habitable zone, light-year, parallax.

Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Earth and Space Science - origins and structure of the universe
Science.prompt: This topic covers cosmology for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand the origin, structure, and evolution of the universe. Big Bang theory: universe began ~13.8 billion years ago from an extremely hot, dense state; evidence includes cosmic microwave background radiation (CMB, discovered 1965), Hubble's law (galaxies recede proportionally to distance: v = H0d), abundance of light elements (hydrogen, helium predicted by Big Bang nucleosynthesis). Universe evolution: initial expansion and cooling → nucleosynthesis (first 3 minutes) → recombination (380,000 years, CMB released) → dark ages → first stars and galaxies → large-scale structure formation. Dark matter: ~27% of universe; does not emit light but affects gravity; evidence from galaxy rotation curves, gravitational lensing, CMB anisotropies. Dark energy: ~68% of universe; causes accelerating expansion; discovered 1998 via Type Ia supernovae. Universe geometry: flat (critical density), open, or closed; observations suggest flat. Future: depends on dark energy behavior (heat death, Big Crunch, Big Rip). Vocabulary: Big Bang, cosmic microwave background, Hubble's law, redshift, dark matter, dark energy, nucleosynthesis, expansion, inflation, cosmological constant, multiverse.

Science.teks_description: Provide examples of atomic and nuclear phenomena applications using the standard model
Science.prompt: This topic covers astrophysics for 12th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.9(D)). Students should apply physics principles to understand astronomical phenomena. Stellar physics: nuclear fusion in stellar cores (proton-proton chain: 4H → He + energy), stellar energy balance (radiation pressure vs. gravitational collapse), stellar luminosity (L = 4πR²σT⁴), mass-luminosity relation (L ∝ M^3.5 for main sequence). Stellar endpoints: white dwarfs (electron degeneracy), neutron stars (neutron degeneracy, pulsars), black holes (Schwarzschild radius rs = 2GM/c², event horizon, singularity). General relativity: gravity as spacetime curvature, gravitational lensing, gravitational waves (detected by LIGO 2015 from merging black holes), GPS corrections. High-energy astrophysics: cosmic rays, gamma-ray bursts, active galactic nuclei, quasars. Compact objects: accretion disks, jets, X-ray binaries. Key equations: escape velocity, orbital mechanics, Doppler effect for radial velocity, parallax for distance measurement. Vocabulary: nuclear fusion, main sequence, white dwarf, neutron star, black hole, event horizon, gravitational wave, general relativity, spacetime curvature, pulsar, quasar, accretion disk.

Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Earth and Space Science - planetary systems
Science.prompt: This topic covers planetary science for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand the formation, composition, and processes shaping planetary bodies. Solar system formation: solar nebula hypothesis — collapse of gas/dust cloud → rotating disk → accretion of planetesimals → protoplanets → clearing of debris. Planetary types: terrestrial (Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars — rocky, dense, thin atmospheres) vs. Jovian (Jupiter, Saturn — gas giants; Uranus, Neptune — ice giants). Planetary interiors: differentiation (heavier elements sink), core, mantle, crust structure. Atmospheres: composition depends on mass, temperature, distance from sun, volcanic activity; greenhouse effect (Venus extreme, Earth moderate, Mars minimal). Surface processes: volcanism, tectonics, erosion, impact cratering, weathering. Comparative planetology: why Earth is habitable (liquid water, magnetic field, moderate greenhouse effect, plate tectonics). Moons and small bodies: tidal heating (Io, Enceladus, Europa), ring systems, asteroids, comets, KBOs, dwarf planets. Exoplanets: thousands discovered; habitable zone, biosignatures, SETI. Vocabulary: accretion, differentiation, terrestrial planet, gas giant, atmosphere, greenhouse effect, habitability, tidal heating, magnetosphere, exoplanet, biosignature.

Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Environmental Systems or Aquatic Science standards
Science.prompt: This topic covers environmental science for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand environmental systems and evaluate human impacts on the natural world. Earth systems: atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, biosphere — interconnected and interdependent. Key environmental issues: climate change (greenhouse gas emissions, global warming, sea-level rise, extreme weather), biodiversity loss (habitat destruction, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, climate change), water resources (freshwater scarcity, pollution, groundwater depletion, ocean acidification), energy (fossil fuel dependence, transition to renewables, nuclear considerations), waste management (solid waste, hazardous waste, e-waste, plastic pollution). Sustainability: meeting present needs without compromising future generations; ecological footprint analysis; circular economy vs. linear economy. Environmental policy: major legislation (Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, ESA, NEPA), international agreements (Paris Agreement, Montreal Protocol). Risk assessment and cost-benefit analysis. Solutions: green technology, conservation strategies, sustainable agriculture, urban planning. Vocabulary: sustainability, ecosystem services, ecological footprint, greenhouse effect, biodiversity, eutrophication, carrying capacity, renewable energy, environmental policy, conservation.

Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Earth and Space Science - geologic processes
Science.prompt: This topic covers geology for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand Earth's structure, composition, and dynamic processes. Earth's structure: crust (oceanic basalt, continental granite), mantle (convection currents), outer core (liquid iron, generates magnetic field), inner core (solid iron). Plate tectonics: lithospheric plates move on asthenosphere; divergent boundaries (mid-ocean ridges, rift valleys), convergent boundaries (subduction zones, mountain building, volcanic arcs), transform boundaries (earthquakes, San Andreas Fault). Evidence: continental fit, fossil distribution, paleomagnetic reversals, seafloor spreading, GPS measurements of plate motion. Rock cycle: igneous (crystallization from magma/lava), sedimentary (compaction and cementation of sediments), metamorphic (heat and pressure transform existing rocks); continuous transformation. Geologic time: relative dating (superposition, cross-cutting relationships), absolute dating (radiometric: half-life calculations), geologic time scale (Eons, Eras, Periods, Epochs). Earth resources: minerals, fossil fuels, groundwater; sustainable extraction. Natural hazards: earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, landslides, tsunamis. Vocabulary: plate tectonics, subduction, convection, rock cycle, igneous, sedimentary, metamorphic, radiometric dating, geologic time, earthquake, volcano, mineral, fossil, stratigraphy.

Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Earth and Space Science - atmospheric science
Science.prompt: This topic covers meteorology for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand atmospheric science, weather patterns, and climate systems. Atmosphere layers: troposphere (weather, temperature decreases), stratosphere (ozone layer), mesosphere, thermosphere, exosphere. Energy budget: solar radiation, albedo, greenhouse effect, radiation balance. Weather factors: temperature, pressure, humidity, wind, precipitation, cloud formation. Air masses and fronts: cold fronts (sharp weather change), warm fronts (gradual change), stationary fronts, occluded fronts. Pressure systems: low pressure (rising air, clouds, precipitation, counterclockwise in NH), high pressure (sinking air, clear skies, clockwise in NH). Severe weather: thunderstorms (convective, requires instability, moisture, lift), tornadoes (rotating thunderstorms, Fujita scale), hurricanes (tropical cyclones, warm ocean water, Saffir-Simpson scale). Global circulation: Hadley, Ferrel, Polar cells; Coriolis effect; trade winds, westerlies, polar easterlies; jet streams. Climate vs. weather: climate is long-term average; climate zones (tropical, temperate, polar); climate change evidence and models. Vocabulary: atmosphere, front, air mass, pressure system, Coriolis effect, jet stream, convection, precipitation, hurricane, tornado, climate, weather, albedo, greenhouse effect.

Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Aquatic Science standards
Science.prompt: This topic covers oceanography for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand ocean systems, their properties, and their role in Earth's environment. Ocean structure: surface zone (warm, mixed by waves), thermocline (rapid temperature decrease), deep zone (cold, dense). Seawater properties: salinity (~35 ppt), temperature, density, dissolved gases, pH. Ocean circulation: surface currents (driven by wind, deflected by Coriolis effect, e.g., Gulf Stream), thermohaline circulation (deep water formation driven by density differences from temperature and salinity, global conveyor belt), upwelling (nutrient-rich cold water rises, high productivity). Waves and tides: wave properties (wind-generated), tsunamis (seismic), tides (gravitational pull of Moon and Sun, spring and neap tides). Ocean floor: continental shelf, slope, rise, abyssal plain, mid-ocean ridges, trenches, hydrothermal vents. Marine ecosystems: coral reefs, open ocean, deep sea, estuaries, intertidal zone; primary productivity by phytoplankton. Human impacts: overfishing, pollution (plastics, oil spills, chemical runoff), ocean acidification, coral bleaching, dead zones. Vocabulary: salinity, thermocline, thermohaline circulation, upwelling, current, tide, continental shelf, abyssal plain, mid-ocean ridge, coral reef, phytoplankton, ocean acidification.

Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Forensic Science course standards
Science.prompt: This topic covers forensic science for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand the application of scientific principles to legal investigations. Crime scene investigation: securing the scene, documentation (photography, sketching, notes), evidence collection (chain of custody), evidence preservation. Physical evidence analysis: fingerprints (latent, patent, plastic; classification by loops, whorls, arches), DNA analysis (STR profiling, PCR amplification, CODIS database), blood evidence (typing, bloodstain pattern analysis, presumptive tests), trace evidence (hair, fibers, glass, soil, pollen), firearms and tool marks (ballistics, rifling, comparison microscopy). Chemical analysis: toxicology (drug and poison detection, blood alcohol), arson investigation (accelerant detection by gas chromatography), drug identification. Biological evidence: entomology (insect succession for time of death), anthropology (skeletal analysis for identification), serology, microbiology. Digital forensics: data recovery, metadata analysis. Key concepts: Locard's exchange principle (every contact leaves a trace), scientific method applied to investigation, expert witness testimony, limitations and reliability of forensic methods. Vocabulary: forensic science, evidence, chain of custody, fingerprint, DNA profiling, ballistics, toxicology, autopsy, Locard's principle, presumptive test, confirmatory test, expert witness.

Science.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 130 Subchapter K: CTE STEM and Engineering courses
Science.prompt: This topic covers engineering for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §130.K). Students should understand the engineering design process and explore various engineering disciplines. Engineering design process: identify the problem, research and gather information, brainstorm solutions, select the best solution, build a prototype, test and evaluate, redesign and iterate. Engineering disciplines: mechanical (machines, mechanisms, thermodynamics), electrical/electronic (circuits, power systems, signal processing), civil (structures, transportation, water systems), chemical (chemical processes, materials, pharmaceuticals), biomedical (medical devices, prosthetics, tissue engineering), aerospace (aircraft, spacecraft, propulsion), computer/software (algorithms, systems, AI), environmental (pollution control, sustainability). Key concepts: constraints (budget, time, materials, safety, environmental), trade-offs, optimization, systems thinking, failure analysis, factor of safety, ethical responsibilities. STEM skills: CAD design, prototyping (3D printing), coding, data analysis, teamwork, technical communication. Engineering connects science and mathematics to practical problem-solving for society. Vocabulary: engineering design process, prototype, iteration, constraint, trade-off, optimization, specification, tolerance, factor of safety, systems thinking, CAD, simulation.

Science.teks_description: Computer Science II (§126.34) or Computer Science III (§126.35) advanced topics
Science.prompt: This topic covers advanced computer science for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §126.34/§126.35). Students should understand advanced programming concepts, data structures, algorithms, and computational thinking. Data structures: arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees (binary, BST, balanced), hash tables, graphs, heaps. Algorithms: sorting (merge sort O(n log n), quicksort, bubble sort O(n²)), searching (binary search O(log n), linear search O(n)), graph algorithms (BFS, DFS, Dijkstra's, A*), dynamic programming, greedy algorithms. Algorithm analysis: Big O notation for time and space complexity, best/average/worst case analysis. Advanced topics: object-oriented programming (classes, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation), recursion, database design (SQL, relational model, normalization), networking basics (TCP/IP, HTTP), cybersecurity fundamentals, AI and machine learning concepts (supervised/unsupervised learning, neural networks). Software development: version control (Git), testing, debugging, documentation, Agile methodology. Key concepts: abstraction, modularity, computational complexity, problem decomposition. Vocabulary: data structure, algorithm, complexity, Big O, recursion, object-oriented, class, inheritance, database, SQL, API, version control, machine learning, neural network.
